title: The Innovation Process
simple_html:
Innovation rarely happens by lucky accident in well-run organisations — it follows a process that can be managed. This lesson shows the basic shape of that process and the most common tool for controlling it.

The core process

A simple way to picture innovation is four steps:

  1. Search — scan for opportunities and threats (new tech, customer needs, market shifts).
  2. Select — choose which ideas to back, given your strategy and resources.
  3. Implement — turn the chosen idea into a real product or service (develop, test, launch).
  4. Capture — get value back (profit, learning) and feed lessons into the next cycle.

This is Tidd and Bessant's Search–Select–Implement–Capture model. It's a loop, not a straight line — you learn and go around again.

The funnel and stage-gate

Because most ideas fail, firms use an innovation funnel: start with many ideas and progressively filter them down. The stage-gate system (Robert Cooper) formalises this — work moves through stages (idea, build, test, launch), and at each "gate" a manager decides go / kill / hold based on clear criteria. This stops weak projects early and saves money.

Key takeaways

  • Innovation is a manageable process, not magic.
  • Search → Select → Implement → Capture, repeated as a learning loop.
  • Use a funnel: many ideas in, few high-quality ones out.
  • Stage-gates force disciplined go/kill decisions at checkpoints.
  • Killing weak ideas early is a feature, not a failure.
intermediate_html:
If innovation is a discipline (Lesson 1), it must have a process that can be designed, measured, and improved. This lesson covers the dominant process models — Tidd and Bessant's generic phases, the innovation funnel, and Cooper's Stage-Gate — and how to choose and run them.

The generic innovation process

Tidd and Bessant (2018) distil innovation into four phases applicable to any sector or innovation type:

  1. Search — scanning the internal and external environment for signals of opportunity and threat (technological, market, regulatory). Good search is wide and continuous.
  2. Select — strategically choosing which signals to act on, given resources, capabilities and risk appetite. This is portfolio management: you cannot pursue everything.
  3. Implement — turning the selected idea into reality: acquiring knowledge, developing the project, launching it, and sustaining adoption.
  4. Capture — realising value (financial returns, strategic learning) and capturing the learning to improve the next cycle.

Critically, it is a learning loop: capture feeds back into search. Treating innovation as linear ("idea to launch and done") is a frequent error.

The innovation funnel

Because uncertainty is high and most ideas do not survive, innovation is managed as a funnel: a wide mouth admitting many divergent ideas that are progressively narrowed through screening and development into a few launchable products. The funnel makes explicit two tasks: generating enough variety at the front end, and applying disciplined selection as projects advance and costs rise.

Stage-Gate

Robert Cooper's Stage-Gate system (Cooper, 1990) is the most widely adopted operationalisation of the funnel. Work proceeds through a sequence of stages (e.g., discovery, scoping, build the business case, development, testing & validation, launch). Between stages sit gates: structured go/kill/hold/recycle decision points where cross-functional gatekeepers evaluate the project against pre-set criteria (strategic fit, technical feasibility, market attractiveness, financial return). The logic is risk and resource management: spend a little to learn a lot, and kill weak projects before they consume large budgets.

Worked example: a consumer-goods firm

A food company runs 40 raw concepts through Gate 1 (a cheap strategic-fit screen); 18 pass to scoping. After Gate 2 (preliminary market and technical assessment), 9 survive to build full business cases. Gate 3 funds 4 for development; prototyping and consumer testing at Gate 4 cuts to 2; one launches. The funnel converted 40 ideas into 1 launch, killing weak projects when they were still cheap to kill — exactly the discipline Stage-Gate is designed to enforce.

Stage-Gate vs. agile/lean alternatives

Classic Stage-Gate is criticised as too linear and slow for high-uncertainty contexts. Modern practice blends it with iterative methods: spiral or Agile-Stage-Gate hybrids embed build-measure-learn loops within stages, and the Lean Startup (Lesson 5) replaces heavy upfront business cases with rapid experimentation when the market is unknown. The rule of thumb: the more radical and uncertain the innovation, the more iterative and experimental the process should be; the more incremental and well-understood, the more a structured Stage-Gate adds value.

How to apply it

  • Map your current pipeline onto Search–Select–Implement–Capture; find the weak phase (often Capture/learning is neglected).
  • Design a funnel with deliberate front-end variety and disciplined gates — don't let pet projects bypass gates.
  • Define explicit gate criteria before projects start to reduce political decision-making.
  • Calibrate process style to innovation type: structured for incremental, experimental for radical.

This process scaffolding connects to opportunity recognition (Lesson 4, the front-end search) and to Lean Startup (Lesson 5, an alternative selection/implementation logic under uncertainty).
full_html:
The innovation process is the managerial backbone of the field: it converts the abstract claim that "innovation is a discipline" (Drucker, 1985) into repeatable, improvable activity. This lesson examines the dominant process models — the generic four-phase model, the innovation funnel, Cooper's Stage-Gate, and their iterative successors — together with the theory of why a process is needed at all (uncertainty reduction and resource discipline) and the debates over linear versus iterative logics.

Why process? Managing uncertainty and variety

Innovation processes exist to manage two facts: outcomes are deeply uncertain, and most ideas fail. The classic empirical estimate is that it takes roughly seven raw ideas to yield one commercial success, with attrition steep at every step (Stevens & Burley, 1997). A process therefore must do two opposed things well — generate variety (divergence) at the front end, and impose selection (convergence) as commitments grow. This is the evolutionary logic of variation-selection-retention applied to management, and it underlies every model below.

The generic process: Search–Select–Implement–Capture

Tidd and Bessant (2018) propose a sector-agnostic four-phase model: Search (environmental scanning for opportunity/threat signals), Select (strategic choice among signals under resource constraints), Implement (acquiring knowledge, executing the project, launching, and sustaining adoption), and Capture (harvesting financial and learning returns and re-investing them). Its theoretical contribution is to insist innovation is a closed learning loop — Capture feeds Search — rejecting the discredited "linear model" in which science pushes inexorably to market. The linear model (technology-push) and its mirror (market-pull) are both incomplete; modern "coupling" and "interactive" models (Rothwell's five generations, Rothwell, 1994) describe innovation as iterative interaction among R&D, marketing, and external networks — culminating in fifth-generation, systems-integrated, networked innovation that anticipates Open Innovation (Lesson 7).

The innovation funnel

The funnel metaphor (Wheelwright & Clark, 1992) visualises development as a narrowing pipeline: a wide mouth admitting many concepts, progressively filtered as information improves and costs rise, yielding a few launches. Effective funnel management balances fill rate (enough quality ideas entering) against screening discipline (rigorous, criteria-based narrowing). A poorly managed funnel exhibits either starvation (too few good ideas) or congestion (too many projects, none adequately resourced — the common "peanut-buttering" pathology).

Stage-Gate

Robert Cooper's Stage-Gate (Cooper, 1990; 2008) is the most widely implemented innovation process, used by a large majority of leading developers. The product moves through cross-functional stages — discovery, scoping, building the business case, development, testing and validation, and launch — separated by gates that act as quality-control and go/kill/hold/recycle decision points. Each gate has three elements: deliverables (what the team brings), criteria (must-meet and should-meet conditions, scored), and outputs (a decision and an approved action plan). The model's strengths are discipline, transparency, parallel cross-functional work, and early termination of weak projects ("fail cheap, fail early"). Cooper himself later evolved it into Stage-Gate Lite and Triple-A (adaptive, agile, accelerated) versions, integrating spiral development and customer feedback loops within stages.

Worked example with attrition economics

Suppose a firm enters 50 concepts at Gate 1 (cost ~$2k each to screen) and applies survival rates of 40%, 50%, 50%, 50% across four gates, with stage costs rising 10x per stage ($20k, $200k, $2M development, $5M launch). Survivors: 50 → 20 → 10 → 5 → ~2 launches. The cumulative screening cost is trivial against avoiding even one full $2M development of a doomed concept. This is the core economic justification for gated processes: the option value of cheap early information dominates. The same logic motivates Lean Startup's even cheaper experiments (Lesson 5) when the uncertainty is about the market rather than the technology.

Linear vs. iterative: the central debate

Stage-Gate is frequently attacked as too linear, bureaucratic, and predicated on knowable requirements — assumptions that hold for incremental, well-understood projects but fail for radical innovation and software, where requirements emerge through experimentation. The contemporary synthesis is contingency-based: choose the process to the uncertainty profile. For low-uncertainty, structured Stage-Gate is efficient. For high market-uncertainty, Lean Startup's build-measure-learn (Ries, 2011) and effectual experimentation (Sarasvathy, 2001; Lesson 4) are superior. For high technical-uncertainty, probe-and-learn and real-options reasoning apply. Many firms now run hybrid Agile-Stage-Gate systems, retaining gates for funding governance while running agile sprints inside development stages (Cooper & Sommer, 2016).

Common mistakes

  • Treating innovation as linear and skipping the Capture/learning loop — the most consequential conceptual error.
  • Gates as rubber stamps: failing to actually kill projects, producing a congested pipeline and "zombie" projects.
  • One-size-fits-all process: applying heavy Stage-Gate to radical or software projects where iteration is essential.
  • Front-end neglect: under-investing in search/idea generation (the "fuzzy front end"), starving the funnel.
  • Confusing activity with progress: measuring inputs (projects, spend) rather than capture (returns, learning).

How to apply

  • Diagnose your pipeline against Search–Select–Implement–Capture and locate the bottleneck phase.
  • Set explicit, scored gate criteria ex ante to depoliticise go/kill decisions.
  • Segment the portfolio by uncertainty and assign the appropriate process (structured vs. iterative vs. hybrid).
  • Instrument the Capture loop: post-launch reviews that feed lessons back into Search.

Connections to the syllabus

The Search phase is operationalised by Lesson 4 (Sources of Innovation & Opportunity Recognition); the Select/Implement phases under high uncertainty are reconceived by Lesson 5 (Lean Startup); fifth-generation networked process foreshadows Lesson 7 (Open Innovation); and Capture/value realisation links to appropriability (Lesson 7) and financing (Lesson 8).
